philosopher.name: Epictetus
philosopher.character_description: Elderly Greek man living in Rome, 60s-70s, short gray beard, balding with wispy white hair, weathered dark skin, one leg slightly lame (walked with a limp), wearing simple undyed wool tunic — not a toga, as he was a freed slave. Intense dark eyes, animated hands when teaching, lean and austere appearance.
philosopher.environment_description: Simple Roman schoolroom or outdoor colonnade, stone benches, modest furnishings, students gathered around, Mediterranean daylight streaming through open arches, Nicopolis setting with Greek-Roman architecture, dusty streets.

character_descriptions.dark_masculine: A scarred, sinewy figure forged by suffering. Dark skin textured like cracked earth, one leg visibly damaged but stance unbroken. Bare-chested with a heavy chain still hanging from one wrist. Eyes burn with defiant fire. Lean, stripped-down, dangerous — a freed slave who could break you with words or hands. Surrounded by shattered chains.

quotes.text: Make the best use of what is in your power, and take the rest as it happens.
quotes.short_version: Use what's in your power. Accept the rest.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man standing at a crossroads, one path clear, the other obscured by storm clouds — he looks only at the clear path.

quotes.text: Seek not that the things which happen should happen as you wish; but wish the things which happen to be as they are, and you will have a tranquil flow of life.
quotes.short_version: Don't wish for things to be different. Wish to want what is.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A river flowing around rocks without resistance — smooth water finding its way through obstacles.

quotes.text: Men are disturbed not by the things which happen, but by the opinions about the things: for example, death is nothing terrible, for if it were, it would have seemed so to Socrates; for the opinion about death, that it is terrible, is the terrible thing.
quotes.short_version: It is not things that disturb us, but our opinions about them.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Two men facing the same storm — one cowering, one standing upright and calm — the storm identical but the men utterly different.

quotes.text: There is only one way to happiness and that is to cease worrying about things which are beyond the power of our will.
quotes.short_version: Happiness has one path: stop worrying about what you can't control.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man releasing a clenched fist, letting something invisible go, his shoulders dropping with relief.

quotes.text: No man is free who is not master of himself.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Chains on the ground next to a man standing tall — the chains are broken but he is the one who broke them.

quotes.text: First say to yourself what you would be; and then do what you have to do.
quotes.short_version: Decide who you are. Then act accordingly.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A craftsman holding a chisel before a block of stone, eyes focused, determined — the sculpture not yet begun.

quotes.text: If you want to improve, be content to be thought foolish and stupid with regard to external things.
quotes.short_version: If you want to grow, accept looking foolish to others.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A student bent over a scroll while others laugh at him — he does not look up.

quotes.text: Whoever is going to listen to the philosophers needs a considerable practice in listening.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Students sitting in attentive silence around a teacher, one student leaning forward with deep concentration.

quotes.text: The philosophers say that the first thing needful is to learn that God exists and provides for all things, and that it is impossible to conceal from him our acts, or even our intentions and thoughts.
quotes.short_version: Nothing is hidden. Not your acts. Not your intentions.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man alone in a dark room holding a candle — light exposing every corner, every shadow eliminated.

quotes.text: He is a wise man who does not grieve for the things which he has not, but rejoices for those which he has.
quotes.short_version: Wise men don't grieve for what's missing. They rejoice in what's present.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man sitting at a simple table with modest food, hands together, expression of genuine contentment.

quotes.text: Of things, some are in our power, and others are not. In our power are opinion, motivation, desire, aversion, and, in a word, whatever is of our own doing. Not in our power are our body, our property, reputation, office, and, in a word, whatever is not of our own doing.
quotes.short_version: Some things are up to us. Some are not. Know which is which.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Two columns side by side — one labeled with things inside a circle of control, one showing things outside it, hands resting on the inner column only.

quotes.text: When you are going about any action, remind yourself what nature the action is. If you are going to bathe, picture to yourself the things which usually happen in the bath: some people splash the water, some push, some use abusive language, and others steal. Thus you will more safely go about this action if you say to yourself, 'I will now go bathe, and keep my own mind in a state conformable to nature.'
quotes.short_version: Before any action, picture what will happen. Go in prepared, not surprised.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man pausing at a doorway, eyes closed briefly in preparation, then opening the door with composure.

quotes.text: Never say about anything, I have lost it; but, I have restored it. Is your child dead? It has been restored. Is your wife dead? She has been restored. Is your estate taken away? Well, and is not that likewise restored?
quotes.short_version: Nothing is lost. It was only ever on loan.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Open hands releasing a bird to the sky — the hands empty but open, not grasping.

quotes.text: Remember that you are an actor in a drama, the nature of which is determined by the author: if short, a short one; if long, a long one. If it is his pleasure you should act a poor man, see that you act it naturally; if the part of a lame man, a ruler, or a private person, do this to your best. For this is your duty, to act well the character assigned you.
quotes.short_version: Play the role you've been assigned — play it well.
quotes.pair_with_visual: An actor in a simple Roman mask, standing in full costume on an empty stage — the theater dark, but he plays his part regardless.

quotes.text: The things that will destroy you are: politics without principle, pleasure without conscience, wealth without work, knowledge without character, business without morality, science without humanity, and worship without sacrifice.
quotes.short_version: There are things that will destroy you. Most men pursue them eagerly.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A grand city skyline slowly being consumed by shadow from the edges inward — the center still bright, unaware.

quotes.text: It is impossible for a man to learn what he thinks he already knows.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man with arms crossed sitting before an open book — the book open but his eyes fixed elsewhere.

quotes.text: If a man is not able to be cheerful, let him be moderate. Or if he cannot be moderate, let him be silent. But if he cannot be silent, let him be gone.
quotes.short_version: If you can't be cheerful, be quiet. If you can't be quiet, leave.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A door slightly ajar, light coming through the crack — the room inside orderly and quiet.

quotes.text: In every affair consider what precedes and follows, and then undertake it.
quotes.short_version: Before you begin anything, consider what comes before and after it.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A general studying a map spread on a table, tracing a route with one finger, seeing beginning and end simultaneously.

quotes.text: Seek not good from without; seek it within yourselves, or you will never find it.
quotes.short_version: Stop looking outside for what can only be found within.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man searching the ground and sky and horizon, while a soft light glows from his own chest — unseen by him.

quotes.text: If you wish to be a writer, write.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A bare desk with only a stylus and wax tablet — no excuses, no audience, just the tool and the work.

quotes.text: Confine yourself to the present.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A sandglass — the sand falling in a single narrow stream, the only moment that matters visible in the hourglass neck.

quotes.text: Any person capable of angering you becomes your master; he can anger you only when you permit him to disturb you.
quotes.short_version: Whoever can anger you owns you.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man pulling another man's strings like a puppet — the puppet's face twisted in rage, unaware of who holds the cords.

quotes.text: If someone speaks badly of you, do not defend yourself against the accusation, but reply: 'You obviously don't know my other faults, or you would have mentioned those as well.'
quotes.short_version: If they criticize you, don't defend. They don't know the half of it.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man smiling calmly at someone pointing an accusing finger at him — the accused completely unruffled.

quotes.text: When you see a man shedding tears in grief for a child abroad or dead, or for loss of property, beware that you are not carried away by the appearance that it is an outward ill that distresses him, and be ready to say at once, 'What distresses this man is not what has happened... but his opinion of it.'
quotes.short_version: His grief is not about the event. It is about what he thinks of the event.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A grieving man and his shadow — the shadow weeping far larger and more dramatically than the man himself.

quotes.text: If your brother wrongs you, remember not to dwell on the wrong he has done, but to think of what you can do to bring your relationship back to its natural state.
quotes.short_version: When someone wrongs you, focus not on the wrong — focus on what you can do to restore.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Two men standing apart, one beginning to take a step toward the other, a bridge half-built between them.

quotes.text: What concerns me is not the way things are, but rather the way people think things are.
quotes.short_version: The problem is never the facts. It is the interpretation.
quotes.pair_with_visual: The same scene painted twice side by side — in one, dark and threatening; in the other, calm and ordinary — identical subject, different perception.

quotes.text: The key is to keep company only with people who uplift you, whose presence calls forth your best.
quotes.short_version: Keep only the company that pulls you upward.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man walking away from a noisy crowd toward a small group of serious men gathered in quiet conversation.

quotes.text: The more we value things outside our control, the less control we have.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man clutching a bag of coins while standing on crumbling ground — tighter grip, more precarious footing.

quotes.text: Never tell people you are a philosopher or talk much with the unlearned about theorems, but do what follows from them. Thus, at a party don't talk about how people ought to eat, but eat as you ought.
quotes.short_version: Don't announce your principles. Live them.
quotes.pair_with_visual: One man talking loudly about virtue at a feast while another man silently acts with virtue — only one is noticed.

quotes.text: If evil be spoken of you and it be true, correct yourself; if it be a lie, laugh at it.
quotes.short_version: If the criticism is true, fix yourself. If it's a lie, laugh.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man looking at his reflection in water — examining it without flinching, adjusting his posture.

quotes.text: Men are not disturbed by the things themselves, but by their opinions about the things. For instance, death is not terrible, since if it were, Socrates would have thought so.
quotes.short_version: Death is not the problem. Your opinion of death is.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A skull on a philosopher's desk beside scrolls and a candle — present but not dominating, just another object in the room.

quotes.text: Whoever does not esteem life cannot be corrupted. But he who sets a high value upon it deprives himself of the power of serving it fully.
quotes.short_version: Cling too tightly to life and you lose the ability to serve it.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man gripping a handful of sand while grain by grain it slips through the gaps in his fingers.

quotes.text: On the occasion of every accident that befalls you, remember to turn to yourself and inquire what power you have for turning it to use.
quotes.short_version: Every setback is a question: what power do you have here?
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man standing before a smashed cart on the road — not looking at the cart but looking at his hands, seeing his tools.

quotes.text: Do not seek for things to happen as you wish, but wish for things to happen as they do, and you will find peace.
quotes.short_version: Peace comes from wanting what happens — not from wanting what you want.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man sitting at sunrise, unchanged from the day before, settled as a stone — the world moving around him in time-lapse.

quotes.text: He is a wise man who does not grieve for the things which he has not, but rejoices for those which he has.
quotes.short_version: Stop counting what's missing. Count what's here.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man at a table with one piece of bread, eating it with the care and attention of a man at a feast.

quotes.text: Freedom is the only worthy goal in life. It is won by disregarding things that lie beyond our control.
quotes.short_version: Freedom is won by releasing what you never controlled.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A bird perched on an open cage door — it has always been free, but only now does it leave.

quotes.text: Seek not that the things which happen should happen as you wish; but wish the things which happen to be as they are, and you will have a tranquil flow of life.
quotes.short_version: Align your wants with reality. That is the whole practice.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A river flowing — smooth where it accepts the banks, turbulent only where it fights the channel.

quotes.text: It is the act of an ill-instructed man to blame others for his own bad condition; it is the act of one who has begun to be instructed, to lay the blame on himself; and of one whose instruction is completed, neither to blame another, nor himself.
quotes.short_version: First you blame others. Then yourself. Then — no one.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Three figures in sequence — first pointing outward, then pointing inward, then standing with open hands pointing at nothing.

quotes.text: Do not explain your philosophy. Embody it.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A craftsman at work, hands doing the task — no audience, no speech, just the embodied act.

quotes.text: Practice yourself, for heaven's sake, in little things; and thence proceed to greater.
quotes.short_version: Start with the small. The great will follow.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A staircase ascending into morning light — the first step worn smooth from use, the higher steps still fresh.

quotes.text: Lameness is an impediment to the leg, but not to the will; and say this to yourself with regard to everything that happens. For you will find such things to be an impediment to something else, but not truly to yourself.
quotes.short_version: Obstacles block the body. The will is never blocked.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man with a lame leg climbing a hill — the leg slows him, but his eyes are fixed on the summit.

quotes.text: When you have shut your doors, and darkened your room, remember never to say that you are alone, for you are not alone; God is within, and your own genius is within.
quotes.short_version: In the dark and alone — you are never actually alone.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man alone in a dark room — but a faint light surrounds him, his own quiet presence visible in the dark.

quotes.text: Be not elated at any excellence not your own. If the horse in his elation were to say, 'I am beautiful,' one could put up with it. But when you say with elation, 'I have a beautiful horse,' know that you are elated at what is the good of the horse.
quotes.short_version: Don't take pride in what belongs to something else.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man boasting beside a horse — the horse magnificent, the man small and ordinary beside it.

quotes.text: Remember that it is not he who gives abuse or blows who affronts, but the view we take of these things as insulting. When, therefore, any one provokes you, be assured that it is your own opinion which provokes you.
quotes.short_version: The insult is never the word. It is the weight you give the word.
quotes.pair_with_visual: An arrow in flight — stopped just before it strikes a man who holds up only his open hand.

quotes.text: Whoever you meet, however deformed or mean they may be in external things, bear in mind that they are your brother or sister.
quotes.short_version: Every person you pass is your brother or sister.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A wealthy man and a beggar on the same road — in the foreground they appear the same height, the same weight.

quotes.text: Difficulties are things that show what men are.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Fire revealing the form of metal being shaped — the heat not destroying but defining.

quotes.text: The world turns aside to let any man pass who knows where he is going.
quotes.short_version: Clarity of purpose clears the road.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A crowd parting instinctively around a single man walking forward with total certainty.

quotes.text: We cannot choose our external circumstances, but we can always choose how we respond to them.
quotes.short_version: Circumstances are not yours. Response is.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Two soldiers in identical rain — one hunched and miserable, one standing straight and untroubled by the same storm.

quotes.text: Ask not that events should happen as you will, but let your will be that events should happen as they do, and you will have peace.
quotes.short_version: Peace is not getting what you want. It is wanting what you get.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man receiving unexpected news — his expression not of surprise or resistance, but of calm acceptance.

quotes.text: He is a man of sense who does not grieve for what he has not, but is glad for what he has.
quotes.short_version: Grief for what's absent. Gladness for what's present. Choose.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Two windows in the same room — one man staring out at the dark sky complaining, another staring at the fire inside grateful.

quotes.text: Wealth consists not in having great possessions, but in having few wants.
quotes.short_version: The wealthy man has fewer wants, not more things.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man in a simple room, sitting in stillness — the room small, but the space inside him immense.

quotes.text: Books are the training weights of the mind.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Stacked scrolls beside an athlete's discus — both instruments of the same kind of work.

quotes.text: Make use of what you have; don't wish for what you don't have.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A carpenter working wood with simple tools — making something extraordinary from ordinary materials.

quotes.text: He who laughs at himself never runs out of things to laugh at.
quotes.pair_with_visual: An old man laughing at his own reflection in still water — genuine, unguarded, light.

quotes.text: Seek not that the things which happen should happen as you wish, but wish that the things which happen may be the things which are, and you will have a peaceful flow of life.
quotes.short_version: Flow with what is, not against what isn't.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A stream navigating through stones, finding its way naturally, not forcing, not stopping.

quotes.text: Nothing great is created suddenly, any more than a bunch of grapes or a fig. If you tell me that you desire a fig, I answer you that there must be time. Let it first blossom, then bear fruit, then ripen.
quotes.short_version: Great things take time. Let them ripen.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A vine at three stages along a wall — bare branch, green cluster, ripe fruit — the sequence unstoppable.

quotes.text: If you want to be a writer, write. If you want to be a philosopher, philosophize. If you want to be a runner, run.
quotes.short_version: You become the thing by doing the thing.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A young man standing at the start of a track at dawn — no audience, just him and the empty road ahead.

quotes.text: Associate with those who will make a better man of you. Welcome those whom you yourself can improve.
quotes.short_version: The right company lifts you. The wrong company buries you.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man choosing a seat at a long table — some seats surrounded by noise and distraction, one seat beside a focused elder.

quotes.text: If you desire to be good, begin by believing that you are wicked.
quotes.short_version: Goodness begins with honesty about where you actually are.
quotes.pair_with_visual: A man looking at his shadow — not running from it, standing over it, studying it.

quotes.text: Neither seek nor shun; take what comes.
quotes.pair_with_visual: Open hands held out, neither grasping nor pushing away — a posture of radical readiness.
